{{count}} confidential workloads are hid: {{count}} confidential workloads are hidden by the active filters._one
{{count}} confidential workloads are hidden by the active filters._other
{{num}} node(s) labeled for TDX — the QG: {{num}} node(s) labeled for TDX — the QGS DaemonSet schedules onto them.
{{ready}}/{{desired}} ready — pods Pendi: {{ready}}/{{desired}} ready — pods Pending usually means the SGX device plugin is missing.
1. Label TEE-capable nodes with the Node: 1. Label TEE-capable nodes with the Node Feature Discovery operator.
2. Enable confidential containers and cr: 2. Enable confidential containers and create a KataConfig to install the kata-cc runtime.
3. Build initdata and deploy a pod with: 3. Build initdata and deploy a pod with runtimeClassName: kata-cc, then verify its attestation from the Workloads list.
A confidential guest is sealed — the con: A confidential guest is sealed — the console cannot exec into it. Add the self-reporting evidence sidecar so the workload publishes a verifiable attestation record (the released secret) the console can read.
A confidential workload proves its hardw: A confidential workload proves its hardware identity to a Trustee Key Broker Service (KBS) at boot; only after the KBS verifies that evidence does it release the workload’s secrets and keys. Each workload runs inside a node’s Trusted Execution Environment, in a cluster — shown here nested as workload → node → cluster.
A KBS resource the guest fetches as proo: A KBS resource the guest fetches as proof it attested — released only after a successful attestation. Use the full path <repository>/<name>/<key>, e.g. default/kbsres1/key1.
A real LUKS helper image is required — t: A real LUKS helper image is required — the placeholder is not a usable image, and leaving it would let Create succeed and then fail at container start. Build/supply an image whose init container opens the LUKS device with the passphrase on boot.
A Trustee-delivered passphrase reference: A Trustee-delivered passphrase reference like kbs:///default/luks/passphrase, or a Kubernetes Secret name.
Add attestation evidence sidecar (self-r: Add attestation evidence sidecar (self-reporting, no exec required)
Adds runAsNonRoot: true so the pod meets: Adds runAsNonRoot: true so the pod meets the restricted Pod Security Standard (allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, dropped capabilities, and the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile are always set). Untick only if your image must run as root.
AMD SEV-SNP — the attestation report is: AMD SEV-SNP — the attestation report is generated in-guest; ensure the Trustee SNP verifier can fetch VCEK collateral from the AMD KDS.
An IOMMU MachineConfig (intel_iommu=on /: An IOMMU MachineConfig (intel_iommu=on / amd_iommu=on) — reboots nodes.
Applying this MachineConfig rolls out a: Applying this MachineConfig rolls out a reboot to every node in the "{{role}}" pool, one at a time.
Attach a raw-block PVC that an init cont: Attach a raw-block PVC that an init container opens with LUKS inside the TEE, using a passphrase Trustee delivers only after attestation.
Attestation happens inside the sealed gu: Attestation happens inside the sealed guest. Deploy the workload with the attestation evidence sidecar to publish a verifiable proof here — a successful secret release confirms the flow above completed.
Attestation infrastructure is deployed a: Attestation infrastructure is deployed and running
Attestation needs the host quote-generat: Attestation needs the host quote-generation stack for your TEE. Without it the guest sends an empty quote and Trustee rejects it — pods still run, but no secret is ever released. Confirm it is deployed for your hardware:
attests to a remote Trustee at {{host}}: attests to a remote Trustee at {{host}}
Available once the kata-cc runtime is in: Available once the kata-cc runtime is installed on your TEE nodes.
Confidential containers are enabled, so: Confidential containers are enabled, so the operator installs the kata-cc runtime.
Confidential containers are not enabled: Confidential containers are not enabled yet
Confidential Containers does not deploy: Confidential Containers does not deploy Trustee — the attestation authority usually runs in a separate, trusted cluster (hub-and-spoke). This view shows the workloads in the current cluster and which Trustee each one actually attests to, read from its initdata: a solid box marked “↳ attests in-cluster” targets a co-located Trustee; a dashed box marked “↗ …” attests to a remote Trustee; “no initdata” means it does not attest at all. The Trustee on the left is reconstructed from those endpoints.
Confidential containers is enabled clust: Confidential containers is enabled cluster-wide. Pick the TEE-capable nodes you intend to run confidential workloads on — placement is per-workload, via the runtime class and a node selector. The snippet below updates as you choose.
Confidential containers run your workloa: Confidential containers run your workload inside a hardware Trusted Execution Environment (Intel TDX or AMD SEV-SNP), so data stays encrypted in use — even from the cluster host. Work through the checklist below to go from a fresh cluster to an attested confidential workload.
Confidential containers run your workload inside a hardware Trusted Execution Environment (Intel TDX or AMD SEV-SNP), so data stays encrypted in use — even from the host. To get started:
Confidential workloads use a kata-cc run: Confidential workloads use a kata-cc runtime class and run inside a hardware TEE. Deploy one with runtimeClassName: kata-cc and initdata to see it here.
Could not enable Intel TDX host: Could not enable Intel TDX host
Could not install the SGX device plugin: Could not install the SGX device plugin
Create a KataConfig with confidential co: Create a KataConfig with confidential containers enabled to install the kata-cc runtime on your TEE nodes. This reboots the selected nodes.
Create a workload with the evidence side: Create a workload with the evidence sidecar
DaemonSet tdx-qgs (per-node quote genera: DaemonSet tdx-qgs (per-node quote generation + platform registration)
Delete {{kind}} {{name}} in {{namespace}: Delete {{kind}} {{name}} in {{namespace}}? Its confidential VM will be torn down.
Deploy a workload with runtimeClassName:: Deploy a workload with runtimeClassName: kata-cc, then use the Verify attestation action on the Workloads list to check it.
Deployment pccs + Service pccs-service (: Deployment pccs + Service pccs-service (PCK certificate cache)
Detect and label your Intel TDX or AMD S: Detect and label your Intel TDX or AMD SEV-SNP nodes with Node Feature Discovery. If a node has TDX enabled in firmware but is not detected, activate the TDX host kernel arguments first. Confidential workloads only schedule onto these nodes.
Enable confidential containers in the Op: Enable confidential containers in the OpenShift sandboxed containers operator — a supported configuration option. The operator then installs the kata-cc runtime on your TEE nodes.
Enable TEE detection and the Intel TDX h: Enable TEE detection and the Intel TDX host first. The snippet below targets any TDX node by label.
Enter the full path <repository>/<name>/: Enter the full path <repository>/<name>/<key> (3 segments), e.g. default/kbsres1/key1. A two-segment path like default/kbsres1 is a folder, not a resource, and returns 404.
HTTPS KBS with no certificate pinned: HTTPS KBS with no certificate pinned
If the error mentions a missing resource: If the error mentions a missing resource, the Node Feature Discovery operator is probably not installed.
Image whose init container opens the LUK: Image whose init container opens the LUKS device with the passphrase on boot.
Initdata comes from your attestation ser: Initdata comes from your attestation service (e.g. Trustee), generated against the KBS your workload will attest to. It can run on this cluster or a remote one (hub-and-spoke). Without it the workload cannot attest.
Installing the Intel Device Plugins Oper: Installing the Intel Device Plugins Operator (this can take a minute)…
Installing the kata-cc runtime reconfigu: Installing the kata-cc runtime reconfigures and reboots the sandboxed-containers nodes, one at a time.
Installing the runtime reconfigures and: Installing the runtime reconfigures and reboots the eligible nodes, one at a time. checkNodeEligibility limits installation to nodes labeled TEE-capable.
Intel TDX — deploy a Quote Generation Se: Intel TDX — deploy a Quote Generation Service (QGS) and a PCCS for PCK collateral (Intel DCAP). The guest reaches the QGS over vsock. Use the guided setup on the right.
Intel TDX must be activated in the host: Intel TDX must be activated in the host kernel before Node Feature Discovery can detect it. This adds the required kernel arguments to the selected MachineConfigPool. Use it when a node has TDX enabled in firmware but is not detected as TEE-capable.
Intel TDX pods prove their identity with: Intel TDX pods prove their identity with a signed TD quote. This deploys the host-side stack that produces and backs those quotes: an in-cluster Provisioning Certificate Caching Service (PCCS) that caches PCK certificates from Intel, automatic per-node platform registration, and a per-node Quote Generation Service (QGS) the guest reaches over vsock. Without it, attestation fails with an empty quote and Trustee never releases a secret.
Intel TDX quote generation is running —: Intel TDX quote generation is running — PCCS (PCK certificate cache) and the per-node QGS are up in the intel-dcap namespace, so TDX pods can produce signed quotes.
It uses a tiny ubi-minimal image and pus: It uses a tiny ubi-minimal image and pushes the evidence to the Kubernetes API with curl (no oc, no python), so it fits inside the confidential guest VM.
Leave blank to auto-select the first con: Leave blank to auto-select the first control-plane node
Match the quote-generation stack to your: Match the quote-generation stack to your TEE — Intel TDX: QGS + PCCS; AMD SEV-SNP: AMD KDS; NVIDIA GPU: NRAS.
Name: Name
Namespace {{name}} does not exist yet an: Namespace {{name}} does not exist yet and will be created.
Namespace intel-dcap and service account: Namespace intel-dcap and service accounts pccs-sa / qgs-sa (privileged SCC)
Namespace, secrets, PCCS and QGS applied: Namespace, secrets, PCCS and QGS applied.
No attestation evidence sidecar on this: No attestation evidence sidecar on this workload
No confidential workloads found in this: No confidential workloads found in this cluster yet.
No nodes are labeled with a Trusted Exec: No nodes are labeled with a Trusted Execution Environment yet. Use Enable TEE detection to set up Node Feature Discovery and label Intel TDX or AMD SEV-SNP nodes.
No results match the current filters: No results match the current filters
No TEE-capable nodes detected. Install t: No TEE-capable nodes detected. Install the Node Feature Discovery operator and a NodeFeatureRule to label TDX / SEV-SNP nodes.
Nodes are labeled by the Node Feature Di: Nodes are labeled by the Node Feature Discovery operator via a NodeFeatureRule: intel.feature.node.kubernetes.io/tdx and amd.feature.node.kubernetes.io/snp mark TEE hardware; nvidia.com/cc.mode.state and nvidia.com/cc.ready.state mark confidential-computing GPUs.
Nodes labeled nvidia.com/cc.mode.state=o: Nodes labeled nvidia.com/cc.mode.state=on, nvidia.com/cc.ready.state=true, and a TEE label.
None detected. Label your TDX nodes firs: None detected. Label your TDX nodes first (Detect TEE nodes), or the QGS has nowhere to run.
Not installed — the QGS stays Pending un: Not installed — the QGS stays Pending until sgx.intel.com/enclave + /provision are advertised:
Note: this runs as a temporary cluster-a: Note: this runs as a temporary cluster-admin Job (namespace, privileged SCC, privileged DaemonSet). The PCCS control-plane node needs outbound access to the Intel PCS, and your Intel TDX MachineConfig must be applied.
NVIDIA GPU — confidential GPU attestatio: NVIDIA GPU — confidential GPU attestation uses NVIDIA NRAS; ensure the Trustee NVIDIA GPU verifier is configured.
One will be created so NFD actually scan: One will be created so NFD actually scans your nodes. This needs the Node Feature Discovery operator — if creating fails, install it from OperatorHub first.
Open the Intel Trusted Services API port: Open the Intel Trusted Services API portal
Open the Job and read its pod logs to se: Open the Job and read its pod logs to see which step failed.
Operator installed — waiting for its adm: Operator installed — waiting for its admission webhook to start…
Optional: a Trustee co-located on this c: Optional: a Trustee co-located on this cluster shared these initdata ConfigMaps in this namespace. Picking one fills the field below; you can still edit or paste your own.
Paste your Provisioning Certification Se: Paste your Provisioning Certification Service API key
PCCS and QGS images come from the pinned: PCCS and QGS images come from the pinned sandboxed-containers-operator {{tag}} manifests.
PCCS is available and the QGS is ready o: PCCS is available and the QGS is ready on {{ready}}/{{desired}} node(s). Intel TDX pods can now produce a signed quote.
PCCS is up, but the QGS DaemonSet is Pen: PCCS is up, but the QGS DaemonSet is Pending: the Intel SGX device plugin is not advertising sgx.intel.com/enclave + /provision yet. Open the setup to install it (TDX quotes are signed by an SGX enclave).
Prefer to run it yourself? Copy the equi: Prefer to run it yourself? Copy the equivalent commands
Pulls the PCCS image and starts on the c: Pulls the PCCS image and starts on the control-plane node.
Red Hat build of Trustee — KBS + Attesta: Red Hat build of Trustee — KBS + Attestation Service
Run a declared container inside the TEE: Run a declared container inside the TEE that continuously fetches a KBS resource to prove attestation and publishes a timestamped evidence record the Trustee plugin reads.
Run these from a workstation with oc log: Run these from a workstation with oc logged in as cluster-admin (needs openssl and envsubst).
Runs on each Intel TDX node once applied: Runs on each Intel TDX node once applied.
Secret pccs-secrets (API key + token has: Secret pccs-secrets (API key + token hashes) and a self-signed PCCS TLS cert
Select a node or a workload to open it.
Set up Intel TDX remote attestation: Set up Intel TDX remote attestation
SGX device plugin created — waiting for: SGX device plugin created — waiting for the node to advertise enclave/provision…
TDX host rollout degraded — check the Ma: TDX host rollout degraded — check the MachineConfigPool
TDX quote generation runs in an SGX encl: TDX quote generation runs in an SGX enclave. The TDX node(s) do not report SGX yet — check firmware and NFD.
TDX quotes are signed by an SGX enclave: TDX quotes are signed by an SGX enclave on the host, and your TDX node(s) report SGX capability (NFD).
The Confidential Containers menu is avai: The Confidential Containers menu is available because sandboxed containers (KataConfig) is installed, but the confidential feature is still off. Enabling it sets confidential: "true" on the osc-feature-gates ConfigMap, so the OpenShift sandboxed containers operator installs the kata-cc runtime on your TEE nodes. This reboots the sandboxed-containers nodes, one at a time.
The Intel TDX attestation infrastructure: The Intel TDX attestation infrastructure is deploying in intel-dcap. It is ready once PCCS is available and the QGS DaemonSet is running on your TDX nodes.
The kata-cc runtime class is created whe: The kata-cc runtime class is created when confidential containers are enabled and a KataConfig is installed on TEE-capable nodes.
The kata-cc-nvidia-gpu runtime needs the: The kata-cc-nvidia-gpu runtime needs the GPU stack enabled on your TEE nodes first (NVIDIA H100, bare metal only):
The node advertises sgx.intel.com/enclav: The node advertises sgx.intel.com/enclave + /provision, so the QGS can schedule.
The NVIDIA GPU Operator with a ClusterPo: The NVIDIA GPU Operator with a ClusterPolicy enabling ccManager (CC mode on), the kata sandbox device plugin, and vfio-manager.
The one manual step. In the Intel Truste: The one manual step. In the Intel Trusted Services API portal, sign in and subscribe to the Provisioning Certification Service; the API key is shown on the Manage Subscriptions page.
The pasted initdata attests to {{host}},: The pasted initdata attests to {{host}}, an in-cluster Service name that only resolves on the cluster that hosts it. If this workload runs on a different (spoke or air-gapped) cluster it cannot reach that KBS and will fail to attest at runtime. Use the attestation service’s external Route URL for cross-cluster workloads. You can still create the workload.
The pasted value didn’t decode to an ini: The pasted value didn’t decode to an initdata.toml — it should be the gzip+base64 cc_init_data your attestation service produced. Check that you copied the whole value. You can still create the workload, but it will not attest.
The PCCS and per-node QGS are applied. N: The PCCS and per-node QGS are applied. New Intel TDX pods can now produce a signed quote; existing pods that failed to attest must be restarted.
The role label of the MachineConfigPool: The role label of the MachineConfigPool holding your TEE nodes — for example "worker", or a custom pool such as "kata-oc".
The setup Job failed — open it below to: The setup Job failed — open it below to read the logs.
The sidecar publishes that record as a C: The sidecar publishes that record as a ConfigMap the Trustee plugin reads in its Attestation status view. It must be present at pod creation; it cannot be added to a running pod.
The sidecar runs inside the TEE as a dec: The sidecar runs inside the TEE as a declared container — not via oc exec, which secure confidential workloads forbid. It proves attestation by fetching a KBS resource through the Confidential Data Hub (the resource is only released after a successful attestation) and pushes a timestamped evidence record to a ConfigMap.
The Trustee endpoint is read from each w: The Trustee endpoint is read from each workload’s initdata.
This creates a KataConfig that installs: This creates a KataConfig that installs the kata-cc runtime on your TEE-capable nodes. Enable confidential containers first — the operator builds the kata-cc runtime from that.
This doesn’t look like valid initdata: This doesn’t look like valid initdata
This enables confidential containers in: This enables confidential containers in the OpenShift sandboxed containers operator — a supported configuration option. The operator then installs the kata-cc runtime on your TEE nodes.
This guest presented valid TEE evidence,: This guest presented valid TEE evidence, Trustee verified it against the reference values, and the KBS released the sealed secret. That release is cryptographic proof of attestation.
This initdata points at an in-cluster KB: This initdata points at an in-cluster KBS
This initdata uses an https:// KBS URL b: This initdata uses an https:// KBS URL but pins no certificate. The in-guest Confidential Data Hub validates the KBS against the certificate baked into initdata, so without one it cannot establish TLS and the workload fails to attest. Regenerate the initdata with the KBS certificate, or point it at the plain-http KBS endpoint. You can still create the workload.
This labels nodes that have a Trusted Ex: This labels nodes that have a Trusted Execution Environment (Intel TDX, AMD SEV-SNP) so confidential workloads can be scheduled on them. The Node Feature Discovery operator does the scanning.
Tokens default to \"mytoken\" if left bl: Tokens default to "mytoken" if left blank, matching the documented example.
Use initdata from Trustee on this cluste: Use initdata from Trustee on this cluster
Waiting for the Job to start.
What this creates in the intel-dcap name: What this creates in the intel-dcap namespace
